Identification.LongTitle: An Act to amend the Criminal Records Act, to make consequential amendments to other Acts and to repeal a regulation
Identification.RunningHead: An Act to amend the Criminal Records Act, to make consequential amendments to other Acts and to repeal a regulation

Provision.Text: This enactment amends the Criminal Records Act to provide for the expiry of criminal records.
It also makes consequential amendments to other Acts and repeals a regulation.

Heading.TitleText: An Act to amend the Criminal Records Act, to make consequential amendments to other Acts and to repeal a regulation

Provision.Text: Her Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows:

Section.Text: The long title of the Criminal Records Act is replaced by the following:

AmendedText.LongTitle: An Act to provide for the expiry of the records of persons who have been convicted of offences

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 1:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the long title:

ExistingText.LongTitle: An Act to provide for the suspension of the records of persons who have been convicted of offences and have subsequently rehabilitated themselves

Subsection.Text: The definitions possession and record suspension in subsection 2(1) of the Act are repealed.

Definition.Text: possessionâmeans possession within the meaning of subsection 4(3) of the Criminal Code;â(possession)
record suspensionâmeans a measure ordered by the Board under section 4.1;â(suspension du casier)

Subsection.Text: Subsection 2(1) of the French version of the Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

Section.Text: Sections 2.1 to 2.3 of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The Board has exclusive jurisdiction and absolute discretion to make an order for a record expiry under section 4.1 or to refuse to make such an order. The Board, in so doing, shall act in accordance with this Act.
The powers, duties and functions of the Board related to an application referred to in section 4.1 shall be exercised by employees of the Board or any class of its employees.

Section.Text: A record expiry

Paragraph.Text: is evidence of the fact that the conviction for an offence in respect of which a record expiry has occurred should no longer reflect adversely on the character of the person who was convicted of the offence; and

requires that the judicial record of the conviction be kept separate and apart from other criminal records and removes any disqualification or obligation that exists, by reason of the conviction, under any Act of Parliament.

Subsection.Text: The Board has exclusive jurisdiction and absolute discretion to order, refuse to order or revoke a record suspension.
The powers, duties and functions of the Board related to an application referred to in subsection 4(3.1) shall be exercised by employees of the Board or any class of its employees.
An application for a record suspension shall be determined, and a decision whether to revoke a record suspension under section 7 shall be made, by a panel that consists of one member of the Board.

The Chairperson of the Board may direct that the number of members of the Board required to constitute a panel to determine an application for a record suspension, to decide whether to revoke a record suspension under section 7 or to determine any class of those applications or make any class of those decisions shall be greater than one.

Section.Text: A record suspension

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: is evidence of the fact that

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the Board, after making the inquiries referred to in paragraph 4.2(1)(b), was satisfied that the applicant was of good conduct, and

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: the conviction in respect of which the record suspension is ordered should no longer reflect adversely on the applicantâs character; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: unless the record suspension is subsequently revoked or ceases to have effect, requires that the judicial record of the conviction be kept separate and apart from other criminal records and removes any disqualification or obligation to which the applicant is, by reason of the conviction, subject under any Act of Parliament other than

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: section 259 of the Criminal Code, as it read immediately before the day on which section 14 of An Act to amend the Criminal Code (offences relating to conveyances) and to make consequential amendments to other Acts comes into force,

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: subsection 147.1(1) or section 227.01 or 227.06 of the National Defence Act,

Subparagraph.Label: (iv)
Subparagraph.Text: section 734.5 or 734.6 of the Criminal Code or section 145.1 of the National Defence Act, in respect of any fine or victim surcharge imposed for any offence referred to in Schedule 3, or

Subparagraph.Label: (v)
Subparagraph.Text: section 36.1 of the International Transfer of Offenders Act.

Section.Text: Subsection 3(1) of the Act and the heading before it are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Subject to sections 4.1 to 4.4, the expiry of the record in respect of an offence for which any of the following persons have been found guilty occurs at the end of the applicable period referred to in section 4:

Paragraph.Text: a person who has been convicted of an offence under an Act of Parliament; and
a Canadian offender, within the meaning of the International Transfer of Offenders Act, who has been transferred to Canada under that Act.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 4:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the heading and subsection 3(1):

Subsection.Text: Subject to section 4, a person who has been convicted of an offence under an Act of Parliament may apply to the Board for a record suspension in respect of that offence, and a Canadian offender, within the meaning of the International Transfer of Offenders Act, who has been transferred to Canada under that Act may apply to the Board for a record suspension in respect of the offence of which he or she has been found guilty.

Section.Text: Section 4 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: The expiry of the record of a person referred to in subsection 3(1) who was an adult at the time that he or she committed the offence referred to in that subsection occurs after the following period has elapsed after the expiration according to law of any sentence â including a sentence of imprisonment, a period of probation and the payment of any fine â imposed for the offence:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: five years, in the case of an offence that is prosecuted by indictment or is a service offence for which the person was punished by a fine of more than $5,000, detention for more than six months, dismissal from Her Majestyâs service, imprisonment for more than six months or a punishment that is greater than imprisonment for less than two years in the scale of punishments set out in subsection 139(1) of the National Defence Act; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: two years, in the case of an offence that is punishable on summary conviction or is a service offence other than a service offence referred to in paragraph (a).

Subsection.Text: If the person referred to in subsection 3(1) was a child at the time when he or she committed the offence, the expiry of his or her record occurs after the expiration according to law of any sentence â including a sentence of imprisonment, a period of probation and the payment of any fine â imposed for the offence.

Subject to subsections (3.1) and (3.11), a person is ineligible to apply for a record suspension until the following period has elapsed after the expiration according to law of any sentence, including a sentence of imprisonment, a period of probation and the payment of any fine, imposed for an offence:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: 10 years, in the case of an offence that is prosecuted by indictment or is a service offence for which the offender was punished by a fine of more than five thousand dollars, detention for more than six months, dismissal from Her Majestyâs service, imprisonment for more than six months or a punishment that is greater than imprisonment for less than two years in the scale of punishments set out in subsection 139(1) of the National Defence Act; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: five years, in the case of an offence that is punishable on summary conviction or is a service offence other than a service offence referred to in paragraph (a).

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (3), a person is ineligible to apply for a record suspension if he or she has been convicted of

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: an offence referred to in Schedule 1; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: more than three offences each of which either was prosecuted by indictment or is a service offence that is subject to a maximum punishment of imprisonment for life, and for each of which the person was sentenced to imprisonment for two years or more.

Subsection.Text: A person who has been convicted of an offence referred to in Schedule 1 may apply for a record suspension if the Board is satisfied that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person was not in a position of trust or authority towards the victim of the offence and the victim was not in a relationship of dependency with him or her;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the person did not use, threaten to use or attempt to use violence, intimidation or coercion in relation to the victim; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the person was less than five years older than the victim.

Subsection.Text: A person who has been convicted only of an offence referred to in Schedule 3 may apply for a record suspension in respect of that offence before the expiration of the period referred to in subsection (1).

A person who has been convicted of an offence referred to in Schedule 3 and other offences may only apply for a record suspension after the expiration of the period referred to in subsection (1), without taking into account any offence referred to in Schedule 3.

A person is ineligible to make an application for a record suspension referred to in subsection (3.1) or (3.11) until after the expiration according to law of any sentence imposed, other than the payment of any fine or victim surcharge, for any offence referred to in Schedule 3.

For greater certainty, subsection (3.2) does not apply to fines and victim surcharges imposed for both an offence referred to in Schedule 3 and for other offences, in which case a person is ineligible to make an application for a record suspension referred to in subsection (3.11) until after all fines and victim surcharges have been paid.

Despite anything in an order made under the Financial Administration Act, a person who makes an application for a record suspension referred to in subsection (3.1) is not required to pay any fee for services provided by the Board in respect of that application if the person has been convicted only of an offence referred to in that subsection.
The person has the onus of satisfying the Board that the conditions referred to in subsection (3) are met.
The person referred to in subsection (3.1) has the onus of satisfying the Board that the person has been convicted only of an offence referred to in that subsection.

For the purpose of an application referred to in subsection (3.1), the Board may not require a person who makes the application to provide a certified copy of information contained in court records in support of the application unless the certified verification of the applicantâs criminal records and information contained in the police records or Canadian Armed Forces records provided in support of the application are not sufficient to demonstrate that the person has been convicted only of an offence referred to in Schedule 3 and that the only sentence imposed for that offence was payment of a fine or victim surcharge or both.
For the purpose of subsection (3.11), a person referred to in that subsection has the onus of satisfying the Board that the person has been convicted of an offence referred to in Schedule 3.
The Governor in Council may, by order, amend Schedule 1 or 3 by adding or deleting a reference to an offence.

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 4.01:

For the purposes of section 4, a reference to the expiration according to law of a sentence of imprisonment imposed for an offence shall be read as a reference to the day on which the sentence expires, without taking into account

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: any period during which the person on whom that sentence was imposed could be entitled to statutory release or any period following a statutory release date; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any remission that stands to the credit of that person in respect of the offence.

Section.Text: Section 4.1 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Record expiry order after review â general rule

Subsection.Text: If the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police reveals that, during the applicable period referred to in section 4, the person was convicted of an offence or that, at the end of that period, there were outstanding charges against the person or the person was under an investigation for an offence, the expiry of that personâs record shall not occur until the date on which the Board orders its expiry in accordance with this section.

The Board shall, within 30 days after the day on which the period referred to in subsection 4(1) ends, notify the person referred to in subsection (1) that the expiry of his or her record has not occurred for the reasons set out in subsection (1) and that he or she may apply to the Board for an order for the expiry of the record.

The Board shall, on application, order the expiry of the applicantâs record if it is satisfied that the record expiry would sustain his or her rehabilitation in society as a law-abiding citizen and would not bring the administration of justice into disrepute.
The applicant has the onus of satisfying the Board that the record expiry would sustain his or her rehabilitation in society as a law-abiding citizen.
In determining whether an order under subsection (3) would bring the administration of justice into disrepute, the Board may consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the nature, gravity and duration of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the circumstances surrounding the commission of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information relating to the applicantâs criminal history and, in the case of a service offence, to any service offence history of the applicant that is relevant to the Boardâs determination; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: any factor that is prescribed by regulation.

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (1.1), the Board may order that an applicantâs record in respect of an offence be suspended, without taking into account any offence referred to in Schedule 3, if the Board is satisfied that

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the applicant, during the applicable period referred to in subsection 4(1), has been of good conduct and has not been convicted of an offence under an Act of Parliament; and

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of an offence referred to in paragraph 4(1)(a), ordering the record suspension at that time would provide a measurable benefit to the applicant, would sustain his or her rehabilitation in society as a law-abiding citizen and would not bring the administration of justice into disrepute.

Subsection.Text: In the case of an application referred to in subsection 4(3.1), the Board shall order that the applicantâs record in respect of that offence be suspended if the applicant has been convicted only of an offence referred to in that subsection and has not been convicted of a new offence under an Act of Parliament, other than an offence referred to in that subsection.
A record suspension ordered under subsection (1.1) may not be revoked by the Board under paragraph 7(b).

In the case of an offence referred to in paragraph 4(1)(a), the applicant has the onus of satisfying the Board that the record suspension would provide a measurable benefit to the applicant and would sustain his or her rehabilitation in society as a law-abiding citizen.
In determining whether ordering the record suspension would bring the administration of justice into disrepute, the Board may consider

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the nature, gravity and duration of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the circumstances surrounding the commission of the offence;

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: information relating to the applicantâs criminal history and, in the case of a service offence, to any service offence history of the applicant that is relevant to the application; and

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: any factor that is prescribed by regulation.

Subsection.Text: Subsections 4.2(1) to (2) of the Act are replaced by the following:

For the purpose of determining whether to order the expiry of a record under section 4.1, the Board may cause inquiries to be made with respect to any factors that it considers necessary to determine whether ordering the record expiry would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.

If the Board proposes to refuse to order a record expiry under section 4.1, it shall notify the applicant of its proposal in writing and advise the applicant that he or she is entitled to make, or have made on his or her behalf, any representations to the Board that he or she believes are relevant either in writing or orally at a hearing held for that purpose.
On receipt of an application for a record suspension, the Board

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: shall cause inquiries to be made to ascertain whether the applicant is eligible to make the application;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: if the applicant is eligible, shall cause inquiries to be made to ascertain the applicantâs conduct since the date of the conviction; and

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: may, in the case of an offence referred to in paragraph 4(1)(a), cause inquiries to be made with respect to any factors that it may consider in determining whether ordering the record suspension would bring the administration of justice into disrepute.

Subsection.Text: The inquiries referred to in paragraph (1)(a), made with respect to an application referred to in subsection 4(3.1) or (3.11), are not to take into account the non-payment of any fine or victim surcharge imposed for any offence referred to in Schedule 3.

The inquiries referred to in paragraphs (1)(b) and (c) do not apply to an application referred to in subsection 4(3.1) and, with respect to all other applications for a record suspension, are not to take into account any offence referred to in Schedule 3.

If the Board proposes to refuse to order a record suspension, it shall notify in writing the applicant of its proposal and advise the applicant that he or she is entitled to make, or have made on his or her behalf, any representations to the Board that he or she believes relevant either in writing or, with the Boardâs authorization, orally at a hearing held for that purpose.
Subsection 4.2(4) of the Act is repealed.
An applicant may not re-apply for a record suspension until the expiration of one year after the day on which the Board refuses to order a record suspension.

Section.Text: Sections 4.3 and 4.4 of the Act are replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Reapplication for a record expiry under section 4.1
Section.Text: A person whose application for a record expiry under section 4.1 is refused may reapply if one year has elapsed since they made the previous application.
Despite section 4.1, a record expiry that did not occur at the end of the applicable period referred to in section 4 for any of the reasons set out in subsection 4.1(1) occurs,

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a record expiry that did not occur because the records retrieval system referred to in subsection 4.1(1) revealed that the person was convicted of another offence during that period, immediately after a record expiry has occurred in respect of that other offence;

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a record expiry that did not occur because the records retrieval system referred to in subsection 4.1(1) revealed that, at the end of the applicable period referred to in section 4, there were outstanding charges against that person or that person was under investigation, immediately after those charges are dropped, the person is acquitted of those charges or the investigation is concluded without charges being brought against the person, as the case may be; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: in the case of a record to which more than one of the reasons set out in subsection 4.1(1) applied, on the later of the events that trigger an expiry referred to in paragraphs (a) and (b).

Section.Text: The Executive Committee shall, after any consultation with Board members that it considers appropriate, adopt operational policies relating to applications for a record expiry following a review under section 4.1, including related inquiries and proceedings.

For the purposes of section 4, a reference to the expiration according to law of a sentence of imprisonment imposed for an offence shall be read as a reference to the day on which the sentence expires, without taking into account

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: any period during which the offender could be entitled to statutory release or any period following a statutory release date; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: any remission that stands to the credit of the offender in respect of the offence.

Section.Text: The Executive Committee shall, after the consultation with Board members that it considers appropriate, adopt policies relating to applications for record suspensions, including related inquiries and proceedings.
Subsections 6(1) to (3) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Section.MarginalNote: Records to be delivered to Commissioner

Subsection.Text: The Minister may, by order in writing addressed to a person having the custody or control of a judicial record of a conviction in respect of which a record expiry has occurred, require that person to deliver that record into the Commissionerâs custody.

A record of a conviction in respect of which an expiry has occurred and that is in the custody of the Commissioner or of any department or agency of the Government of Canada shall be kept separate and apart from other criminal records.

The Minister may, by order in writing addressed to a person having the custody or control of a judicial record of a conviction in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered, require that person to deliver that record into the Commissionerâs custody.

A record of a conviction in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered that is in the custody of the Commissioner or of any department or agency of the Government of Canada shall be kept separate and apart from other criminal records. Subject to subsection (2.1), no record of a conviction is to be disclosed to any person, nor is the existence of the record or the fact of the conviction to be disclosed to any person, without the prior approval of the Minister.

The prior approval of the Minister referred to in subsection (2) is not necessary for the purposes of sections 734.5 and 734.6 of the Criminal Code or section 145.1 of the National Defence Act for non-payment of a fine or victim surcharge that is imposed for an offence referred to in Schedule 3.

The Minister shall, before granting the approval for disclosure referred to in subsection (2), satisfy himself that the disclosure is desirable in the interests of the administration of justice or for any purpose related to the safety or security of Canada or any state allied or associated with Canada.

Section.Text: Subsection 6.1(2) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsection (3), no record of a conviction for an offence in respect of which an expiry has occurred and that is in the custody of the Commissioner or any department or agency of the Government of Canada shall be disclosed to any person, nor shall the existence of the record be disclosed to any person, without the prior approval of the Minister.

The prior approval of the Minister is not required for the purposes of sections 734.5 and 734.6 of the Criminal Code or section 145.1 of the National Defence Act in respect of the non-payment of a fine or victim surcharge that has been imposed for an offence.

No record of a conviction for an offence that no longer constitutes an offence after a person was convicted of that offence and that is in the custody of the Commissioner or any department or agency of the Government of Canada shall be disclosed to any person, nor shall the existence of the record be disclosed to any person, without the prior approval of the Minister.

The Minister shall, before granting approval for a disclosure referred to in subsections (2) and (4), satisfy himself or herself that the disclosure is desirable in the interests of the administration of justice or for any purpose related to the safety or security of Canada or any state allied or associated with Canada.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Record not to be disclosed unless in CPIC
Subsection.Text: No record of a conviction for an offence in respect of which an expiry has not occurred shall be disclosed to any person, nor shall the existence of the record be disclosed to any person, unless the record is included on the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Purging CPIC â discharges and offences referred to in subsection (4)
Subsection.Text: The Commissioner shall remove all references to a discharge under section 730 of the Criminal Codeor to a record of a conviction for an offence referred to in subsection (4) from the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police

Paragraph.Text: on the expiration of the relevant period referred to in subsection (1) in the case of the record of a discharge; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: without delay after the offence no longer constitutes an offence.

Subsection.MarginalNote: Purging CPIC â offences in respect of which a record expired
Subsection.Text: The Commissioner shall remove from the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police all references to an offence if a record expiry has occurred in respect of that offence. The Commissioner shall remove those references without delay after the record expiry has occurred.

For the purposes of subsections (4) and (7), offence that no longer constitutes an offence means an offence in respect of an activity that no longer constitutes an offence under an Act of Parliament or an offence provided for in a provision that has been found unconstitutional by a court of appeal when that courtâs decision is final or has been confirmed by a higher court.

The Commissioner shall remove all references to a discharge under section 730 of the Criminal Code from the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police on the expiration of the relevant period referred to in subsection (1).

Section.Text: The portion of section 6.2 of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

Despite sections 6 and 6.1, the name, date of birth and last known address of a person in respect of whom a record expiry has occurred or who has received a discharge referred to in section 6.1 may be disclosed to a police force if a fingerprint, identified as that of the person, is found

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 12:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portion of section 6.2:

Section.Text: Despite sections 6 and 6.1, the name, date of birth and last known address of a person whose record is suspended under section 4.1 or who has received a discharge referred to in section 6.1 may be disclosed to a police force if a fingerprint, identified as that of the person, is found

Subsection.Text: Subsection 6.3(2) of the Act is replaced by the following:

) The Commissioner shall make, in the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, a notation enabling a member of a police force or other authorized body to determine whether there is a record of an individualâs conviction for an offence against a vulnerable person in respect of which a record expiry has occurred.

The Commissioner shall make, in the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, a notation enabling a member of a police force or other authorized body to determine whether there is a record of an individualâs conviction for an offence listed in Schedule 2 in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered.
Subsection 6.3(9) of the Act is repealed.
The Governor in Council may, by order, amend Schedule 2 by adding or deleting a reference to an offence.

Section.Text: Section 6.4 of the Act is replaced by the following:
Section 6.3 applies in respect of a record of a conviction for any offence in respect of which a record has expired, regardless of the date of the conviction.
Section 6.3 applies in respect of a record of a conviction for any offence in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered regardless of the date of the conviction.
The heading before section 7 of the Act and sections 7 to 7.2 of the Act are repealed.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 15:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of the heading and sections 7 to 7.2 :

Section.Text: A record suspension may be revoked by the Board

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: if the person to whom it relates is subsequently convicted of an offence referred to in paragraph 4(1)(b), other than an offence referred to in subparagraph 7.2(a)(ii);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: on evidence establishing to the satisfaction of the Board that the person to whom it relates is no longer of good conduct; or

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: on evidence establishing to the satisfaction of the Board that the person to whom it relates knowingly made a false or deceptive statement in relation to the application for the record suspension, or knowingly concealed some material particular in relation to that application.

Subsection.Text: If the Board proposes to revoke a record suspension, it shall notify in writing the person to whom it relates of its proposal and advise that person that he or she is entitled to make, or have made on his or her behalf, any representations to the Board that he or she believes relevant either in writing or, with the Boardâs authorization, orally at a hearing held for that purpose.
The Board shall, before making its decision, consider any representations made to it within a reasonable time after the notification is given to a person under subsection (1).

Section.Text: A record suspension ceases to have effect if

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the person to whom it relates is subsequently convicted of

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: an offence referred to in paragraph 4(1)(a), or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: an offence under the Criminal Code, other than an offence under subsection 320.14(1) or 320.15(1) of that Act, the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act, the Firearms Act, Part III or IV of the Food and Drugs Act or the Narcotic Control Act, chapter N-1 of the Revised Statutes of Canada, 1985, that is punishable either on conviction on indictment or on summary conviction; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the Board is convinced by new information that the person was not eligible for the record suspension when it was ordered.

Section.Text: The portion of section 8 of the Act before paragraph (a) is replaced by the following:

No person shall use or authorize the use of an application form for or relating to any of the following matters that contains a question that by its terms requires the applicant to disclose a conviction in respect of which a record expiry has occurred:

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 16:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portion of section 8:

Section.Text: No person shall use or authorize the use of an application form for or relating to any of the following matters that contains a question that by its terms requires the applicant to disclose a conviction in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered and has not been revoked or ceased to have effect:
Section 9 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Nothing in this Act in any manner limits or affects Her Majestyâs royal prerogative of mercy or the provisions of the Criminal Code relating to pardons, except that section 6, subsections 6.1(2), (3), (5) and (8) and section 8 apply in respect of any pardon granted pursuant to the royal prerogative of mercy or those provisions.

Nothing in this Act in any manner limits or affects Her Majestyâs royal prerogative of mercy or the provisions of the Criminal Code relating to pardons, except that sections 6 and 8 apply in respect of any pardon granted pursuant to the royal prerogative of mercy or those provisions.
Section 9.01 of the Act is replaced by the following:

The Board may disclose decisions that order or refuse to order a record expiry under section 4.1. However, it may not disclose information that could reasonably be expected to identify an individual unless the individual authorizes the disclosure in writing.

The Board may disclose decisions that order or refuse to order record suspensions. However, it may not disclose information that could reasonably be expected to identify an individual unless the individual authorizes the disclosure in writing.
Paragraph 9.1(c.1) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c.1)
Paragraph.Text: prescribing the manner in which a person can make an application for the purposes of subsection 4.1(2);
prescribing factors for the purposes of paragraph 4.1(5)(d);

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 19:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Text of relevant portions of section 9.1:

Section.Text: The Governor in Council may make regulations

Paragraph.Label: (c.1)
Paragraph.Text: prescribing factors for the purposes of paragraph 4.1(3)(d);

Section.Text: The Act is amended by adding the following after section 9.1:

Section.MarginalNote: Applications free of fee or charge
Section.Text: No fee or charge may be imposed on any person referred to in subsection 3(1) for an application made under section 4.1.
Paragraphs 11(1)(a) to (c) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the number of applications for a record expiry following a review under section 4.1 made in respect of the offences referred to in each of paragraphs 4(1)(a) and (b);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the number of record expiries following a review under section 4.1 that the Board ordered in respect of the offences referred to in each of paragraphs 4(1)(a) and (b);

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the number of record expiries following a review under section 4.1 ordered, categorized by the offence to which they relate and, if applicable, the province of residence of the applicant; and

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 21:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of subsection 11(1):

Subsection.Text: The Board shall, within three months after the end of each fiscal year, submit to the Minister a report for that year containing the following information:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: the number of applications for record suspensions made in respect of the offences referred to in each of paragraphs 4(1)(a) and (b);

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: the number of record suspensions that the Board ordered or refused to order, in respect of the offences referred to in each of paragraphs 4(1)(a) and (b);

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the number of record suspensions ordered, categorized by the offence to which they relate and, if applicable, the province of residence of the applicant; and

Section.Text: Schedule 1 to the Act is repealed.

Provision.Text: (Subsections 4(2), (3) and (5))
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 151 (sexual interference with a person under 16),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 152 (invitation to a person under 16 to sexual touching),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 153 (sexual exploitation of a person 16 or more but under 18),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: subsection 160(3) (bestiality in the presence of a person under 16 or inciting a person under 16 to commit bestiality),

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: section 163.1 (child pornography),

Provision.Label: (vi)
Provision.Text: section 170 (parent or guardian procuring sexual activity),

Provision.Label: (vii)
Provision.Text: section 171 (householder permitting sexual activity),

Provision.Label: (vii.1)
Provision.Text: paragraph 171.1(1)(a) (making sexually explicit material available to child under 18 for purposes of listed offences),

Provision.Label: (vii.2)
Provision.Text: paragraph 171.1(1)(b) (making sexually explicit material available to child under 16 for purposes of listed offences),

Provision.Label: (vii.3)
Provision.Text: paragraph 171.1(1)(c) (making sexually explicit material available to child under 14 for purposes of listed offences),

Provision.Label: (viii)
Provision.Text: section 172 (corrupting children),

Provision.Label: (ix)
Provision.Text: section 172.1 (luring a child),

Provision.Label: (ix.1)
Provision.Text: paragraph 172.2(1)(a) (agreement or arrangement â listed sexual offence against child under 18),

Provision.Label: (ix.2)
Provision.Text: paragraph 172.2(1)(b) (agreement or arrangement â listed sexual offence against child under 16),

Provision.Label: (ix.3)
Provision.Text: paragraph 172.2(1)(c) (agreement or arrangement â listed sexual offence against child under 14),

Provision.Label: (x)
Provision.Text: subsection 173(2) (exposure),

Provision.Label: (xi) to (xiii)
Provision.Text: [Repealed, 2014, c. 25, s. 35]

Provision.Label: (xiv)
Provision.Text: paragraph 273.3(1)(a) (removal of child under 16 from Canada for purposes of listed offences),

Provision.Label: (xv)
Provision.Text: paragraph 273.3(1)(b) (removal of child 16 or more but under 18 from Canada for purpose of listed offence),

Provision.Label: (xvi)
Provision.Text: paragraph 273.3(1)(c) (removal of child under 18 from Canada for purposes of listed offences),

Provision.Label: (xvi.1)
Provision.Text: section 279.011 (trafficking â person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvi.2)
Provision.Text: subsection 279.02(2) (material benefit â trafficking of person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvi.3)
Provision.Text: subsection 279.03(2) (withholding or destroying documents â trafficking of person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvi.4)
Provision.Text: subsection 286.1(2) (obtaining sexual services for consideration from person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvi.5)
Provision.Text: subsection 286.2(2) (material benefit from sexual services provided by person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvi.6)
Provision.Text: subsection 286.3(2) (procuring â person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (xvii)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(a) with respect to breaking and entering a place with intent to commit in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xvi), and

Provision.Label: (xviii)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(b) with respect to breaking and entering a place and committing in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xvi);

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1970, c. C-34, as that Act read before January 1, 1988:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: subsection 146(1) (sexual intercourse with a female under 14),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: subsection 146(2) (sexual intercourse with a female 14 or more but under 16),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 151 (seduction of a female 16 or more but under 18),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: section 166 (parent or guardian procuring defilement), and

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: section 167 (householder permitting defilement);

Provision.Label: (b.1)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, as they read from time to time before the day on which this paragraph comes into force:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: subsection 212(2) (living on the avails of prostitution of person under 18 years),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: subsection 212(2.1) (aggravated offence in relation to living on the avails of prostitution of person under 18 years), and

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: subsection 212(4) (prostitution of person under 18 years);

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: that are referred to in paragraph (a) and that are punishable under section 130 of the National Defence Act;

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: that are referred to in paragraph (b) and that are punishable under section 120 of the National Defence Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. N-4; and

Provision.Label: (e)
Provision.Text: of attempt or conspiracy to commit an offence referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (d).
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: involving a child under the following provisions of the Criminal Code:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 153.1 (sexual exploitation of a person with a disability),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 155 (incest),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 162 (voyeurism),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: subsection 163(1) (obscene materials),

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: paragraph 163(2)(a) (obscene materials),

Provision.Label: (vi)
Provision.Text: section 168 (mailing obscene matter),

Provision.Label: (vii)
Provision.Text: subsection 173(1) (indecent acts),

Provision.Label: (viii)
Provision.Text: section 271 (sexual assault),

Provision.Label: (ix)
Provision.Text: subsection 272(1) and paragraph 272(2)(a) (sexual assault with firearm),

Provision.Label: (x)
Provision.Text: subsection 272(1) and paragraph 272(2)(b) (sexual assault other than with firearm),

Provision.Label: (xi)
Provision.Text: section 273 (aggravated sexual assault),

Provision.Label: (xii)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(a) with respect to breaking and entering a place with intent to commit in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xi), and

Provision.Label: (xiii)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(b) with respect to breaking and entering a place and committing in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xi);

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: involving a child under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1970, c. C-34, as that Act read before January 1, 1988:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 153 (sexual intercourse with stepdaughter, etc., or female employee), and

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 157 (gross indecency);

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: involving a child under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1970, c. C-34, as that Act read before January 4, 1983:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 144 (rape),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 145 (attempt to commit rape),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 149 (indecent assault on female),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: section 156 (indecent assault on male),

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: section 245 (common assault), and

Provision.Label: (vi)
Provision.Text: subsection 246(1) (assault with intent to commit an indictable offence);

Provision.Label: (d)
Provision.Text: that are referred to in paragraph (a) and that are punishable under section 130 of the National Defence Act;

Provision.Label: (e)
Provision.Text: that are referred to in paragraph (b) or (c) and that are punishable under section 120 of the National Defence Act, R.S.C. 1970, c. N-4; and

Provision.Label: (f)
Provision.Text: of attempt or conspiracy to commit an offence referred to in any of paragraphs (a) to (e).
[Repealed, 2012, c. 1, s. 133]

Section.Text: Schedule 2 to the Act is repealed

Provision.Text: (Subsections 6.3(2) and (9))
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 153.1 (sexual exploitation of a person with a disability),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 155 (incest),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 162 (voyeurism),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: subsection 163(1) (obscene materials),

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: paragraph 163(2)(a) (obscene materials),

Provision.Label: (vi)
Provision.Text: section 168 (mailing obscene matter),

Provision.Label: (vii)
Provision.Text: subsection 173(1) (indecent acts),

Provision.Label: (viii)
Provision.Text: section 271 (sexual assault),

Provision.Label: (ix)
Provision.Text: subsection 272(1) and paragraph 272(2)(a) (sexual assault with firearm),

Provision.Label: (x)
Provision.Text: subsection 272(1) and paragraph 272(2)(b) (sexual assault other than with firearm),

Provision.Label: (xi)
Provision.Text: section 273 (aggravated sexual assault),

Provision.Label: (xii)
Provision.Text: section 280 (abduction of a person under 16),

Provision.Label: (xiii)
Provision.Text: section 281 (abduction of a person under 14),

Provision.Label: (xiv)
Provision.Text: subsection 372(2) (indecent phone calls),

Provision.Label: (xv)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(a) with respect to breaking and entering a place with intent to commit in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xiv), and

Provision.Label: (xvi)
Provision.Text: paragraph 348(1)(b) with respect to breaking and entering a place and committing in that place an indictable offence listed in any of subparagraphs (i) to (xiv); and

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: of attempt or conspiracy to commit an offence referred to in any of subparagraphs (a)(i) to (xvi).
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1970, c. C-34, as that Act read before January 1, 1988:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: subsection 146(1) (sexual intercourse with a female under 14),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: subsection 146(2) (sexual intercourse with a female 14 or more but under 16),

Provision.Label: (iii)
Provision.Text: section 151 (seduction of a female 16 or more but under 18),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: section 153 (sexual intercourse with stepdaughter, etc., or female employee), and

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: section 157 (gross indecency); and

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: of attempt or conspiracy to commit an offence referred to in any of subparagraphs (a)(i) to (v).
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: under the following provisions of the Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1970, c. C-34, as that Act read before January 4, 1983:

Provision.Label: (i)
Provision.Text: section 144 (rape),

Provision.Label: (ii)
Provision.Text: section 145 (attempt to commit rape),

Provision.Label: (iiii)
Provision.Text: section 149 (indecent assault on female),

Provision.Label: (iv)
Provision.Text: section 156 (indecent assault on male),

Provision.Label: (v)
Provision.Text: section 245 (common assault), and

Provision.Label: (vi)
Provision.Text: subsection 246(1) (assault with intent to commit an indictable offence); and

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: of attempt or conspiracy to commit an offence referred to in any of subparagraphs (a)(i) to (vi).

Section.Text: Schedule 3 to the Act is repealed.

Provision.Text: (Subsections 4(3.1) and (5))
Cannabis Offences
Offences

Provision.Label: (a)
Provision.Text: under subsection 4(4) or (5) of the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act, as it read from time to time before October 17, 2018, for possession of a substance included in Item 1 of Schedule II to that Act, except for similar synthetic preparations of cannabis, other than any substance that is identical to any phytocannabinoid produced by, or found in, a cannabis (marihuana) plant, regardless of how the substance was obtained;

Provision.Label: (b)
Provision.Text: under subsection 3(2) of the Narcotic Control Act, chapter N-1 of the Revised Statutes of Canada, 1985, as it read from time to time before May 14, 1997, for possession of a substance included in Item 3 of the schedule to that Act, except for similar synthetic preparations of cannabis sativa, other than any substance that is identical to any phytocannabinoid produced by, or found in, a cannabis (marihuana) plant, regardless of how the substance was obtained; and

Provision.Label: (c)
Provision.Text: under the National Defence Act or any previous version of that Act for an act or omission that constitutes an offence listed in paragraph (a) or (b).

Section.Text: A record expiry is deemed to have occurred in accordance with the Criminal Records Act, as amended by this Act, in respect of all criminal records for which a suspension was granted under the Criminal Records Act as it read immediately before the day on which this Act comes into force.

Any application for a record suspension that was made under the Criminal Records Act before the day on which this Act comes into force and that was not finally disposed of on that day shall be dealt with and disposed of in accordance with the following provisions:

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: sections 4 to 4.02 of the Criminal Records Act, as enacted by this Act, if

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the applicant was not convicted of any subsequent offence after the day on which he or she was convicted of the offence for which he or she sought a record suspension, and

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: on the day on which this Act comes into force, the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police does not reveal that there are outstanding charges against the applicant or that he or she is under investigation; or

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: sections 4.1 to 4.4 of the Criminal Records Act, as enacted by this Act, if

Subparagraph.Label: (i)
Subparagraph.Text: the applicant was convicted of any subsequent offence after he or she was convicted of the offence for which he or she sought a record suspension, or

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: on the day on which this Act comes into force, the automated criminal conviction records retrieval system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police reveals that there are outstanding charges against the applicant or that he or she is under investigation for an offence.

Subsection.Text: The definition record suspension in subsection 490.011(1) of the Criminal Code is repealed.

Definition.Text: record suspension means a record suspension, as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Criminal Records Act, that has not been revoked or ceased to have effect. (suspension du casier)

Subsection.Text: Subsection 490.011(1) of the Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

Definition.Text: record expiry means a record expiry under the Criminal Records Act. (expiration du casier)

Subsection.Text: Subsection 490.015(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:
Despite subsections (1) and (2), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred.
Subsection 490.015(5) of the Act is replaced by the following:

A person whose application is refused may reapply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also reapply once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred. However, they may not reapply under this subsection if an order is made with respect to them under section 490.012 of this Act or section 227.01 of the National Defence Act after the previous application was made.
Despite subsections (1) and (2), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered.

A person whose application is refused may re-apply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also re-apply once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered. However, they may not re-apply under this subsection if an order is made with respect to them under section 490.012 of this Act or section 227.01 of the National Defence Act after the previous application was made.

Section.Text: Paragraph 490.022(2)(c) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the day on which a person referred to in paragraph 490.02(1)(b) provides satisfactory proof of a pardon or a record expiry to a person who collects information, as defined in subsection 3(1) of the Sex Offender Information Registration Act, at a registration centre.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 29:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of subsection 490.022(2):

Subsection.Text: The obligation ends on the earliest of

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the day on which a person referred to in paragraph 490.02(1)(b) provides satisfactory proof of a pardon or record suspension to a person who collects information, as defined in subsection 3(1) of the Sex Offender Information Registration Act, at a registration centre.

Section.Text: Subsections 490.026(4) and (5) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Despite subsections (2) and (3), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred.

A person whose application is refused may reapply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also reapply once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred. However, they may not reapply if, after the previous application was made, they become subject to an obligation under section 490.02901, under section 227.06 of the National Defence Act or under section 36.1 of the International Transfer of Offenders Act or to an order under section 490.012 or under section 227.01 of the National Defence Act.
Despite subsections (2) and (3), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered.

A person whose application is refused may apply again if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also apply again once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered. However, they may not apply again if, after the previous application was made, they become subject to an obligation under section 490.02901, under section 227.06 of the National Defence Act or under section 36.1 of the International Transfer of Offenders Act or to an order under section 490.012 or under section 227.01 of the National Defence Act.

Section.Text: Paragraph 638(1)(c) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a juror has been convicted of an offence for which they were sentenced to a term of imprisonment of two years or more and for which no pardon or record expiry has occurred;

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 31:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of subsection 638(1):

Subsection.Text: A prosecutor or an accused is entitled to any number of challenges on the ground that

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a juror has been convicted of an offence for which they were sentenced to a term of imprisonment of two years or more and for which no pardon or record suspension is in effect;

Section.Text: Paragraph 672.35(c) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may take the verdict into account in considering an application by the accused for parole or any application under the Criminal Records Act in respect of any other offence.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 32:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of section 672.35:

Section.Text: Where a verdict of not criminally responsible on account of mental disorder is rendered, the accused shall not be found guilty or convicted of the offence, but

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may take the verdict into account in considering an application by the accused for parole or for a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act in respect of any other offence.

Section.Text: Subsection 750(4) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: A person to whom subsection (3) applies may, at any time before a record expiry occurs under the Criminal Records Act, apply to the Governor in Council for the restoration of one or more of the capacities lost by the person by virtue of that subsection.

A person to whom subsection (3) applies may, at any time before a record suspension for which he or she has applied is ordered under the Criminal Records Act, apply to the Governor in Council for the restoration of one or more of the capacities lost by the person by virtue of that subsection.

Section.Text: Section 2 of the English version of the Canadian Human Rights Act is replaced by the following:

The purpose of this Act is to extend the laws in Canada to give effect, within the purview of matters coming within the legislative authority of Parliament, to the principle that all individuals should have an opportunity equal with other individuals to make for themselves the lives that they are able and wish to have and to have their needs accommodated, consistent with their duties and obligations as members of society, without being hindered in or prevented from doing so by discriminatory practices based on race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, marital status, family status, genetic characteristics, disability or conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record expiry has occurred.

The purpose of this Act is to extend the laws in Canada to give effect, within the purview of matters coming within the legislative authority of Parliament, to the principle that all individuals should have an opportunity equal with other individuals to make for themselves the lives that they are able and wish to have and to have their needs accommodated, consistent with their duties and obligations as members of society, without being hindered in or prevented from doing so by discriminatory practices based on race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, marital status, family status, genetic characteristics, disability or conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered.
Subsection 3(1) of the English version of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: For all purposes of this Act, the prohibited grounds of discrimination are race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, marital status, family status, genetic characteristics, disability and conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record expiry has occurred.

For all purposes of this Act, the prohibited grounds of discrimination are race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, age, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, marital status, family status, genetic characteristics, disability and conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered.

Section.Text: The definition conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered in section 25 of the Act is replaced by the following:

Definition.Text: conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record expiry has occurred means a conviction of an individual for an offence in respect of which

Paragraph.Text: a pardon has been granted under Her Majestyâs royal prerogative of mercy or under section 748 of the Criminal Code; or
a record expiry has occurred under the Criminal Records Act; (Ã©tat de personne graciÃ©e)

Definition.Text: conviction for an offence for which a pardon has been granted or in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered means a conviction of an individual for an offence in respect of which a pardon has been granted under Her Majestyâs royal prerogative of mercy or under section 748 of the Criminal Code or a record suspension has been ordered under the Criminal Records Act, that has not been revoked or ceased to have effect; (Ã©tat de personne graciÃ©e)

Section.Text: Paragraph 202.14(2)(h) of the National Defence Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may take the finding into account in considering an application by that person for parole or any application under the Criminal Records Act in respect of any other offence.

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 37:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of subsection 202.14(2):

Subsection.Text: Where a finding of not responsible on account of mental disorder is made, the accused person shall not be found guilty or convicted of the offence, but

Paragraph.Label: (h)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may take the finding into account in considering an application by that person for parole or for a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act in respect of any other offence.

Subsection.Text: The definition record suspension in section 227 of the Act is repealed.

Definition.Text: record suspension means a record suspension, as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Criminal Records Act, that has not been revoked or ceased to have effect. (suspension du casier)

Subsection.Text: Section 227 of the Act is amended by adding the following in alphabetical order:

Definition.Text: record expiry means a record expiry under the Criminal Records Act. (expiration du casier)

Subsection.Text: Subsection 227.03(3) of the Act is replaced by the following:
Despite subsections (1) and (2), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred.
Despite subsections (1) and (2), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered.

A person whose application is refused may re-apply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also re-apply once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered. However, they may not re-apply under this subsection if an order is made with respect to them under section 227.01 of this Act or section 490.012 of the Criminal Code after the previous application was made.
Subsection 227.03(5) of the Act is replaced by the following:

A person whose application is refused may reapply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also reapply once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred. However, they may not reapply under this subsection if an order is made with respect to them under section 227.01 of this Act or section 490.012 of the Criminal Code after the previous application was made.

Section.Text: Subsections 227.12(4) and (5) of the Act are replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Despite subsections (2) and (3), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred.

A person whose application is refused may reapply if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also reapply once they receive a pardon or once a record expiry has occurred. However, they may not reapply if, after the previous application was made, they become subject to an obligation under section 490.019 or 490.02901 of the Criminal Code or under section 36.1 of the International Transfer of Offenders Act or to an order under section 227.01 of this Act or section 490.012 of the Criminal Code.
Despite subsections (2) and (3), a person may apply for a termination order once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered.

A person whose application is refused may apply again if five years have elapsed since they made the previous application. They may also apply again once they receive a pardon or once a record suspension is ordered. However, they may not apply again if, after the previous application was made, they become subject to an obligation under section 490.019 or 490.02901 of the Criminal Code or under section 36.1 of the International Transfer of Offenders Act or to an order under section 227.01 of this Act or section 490.012 of the Criminal Code.
Subparagraph (a)(ii) of the definition ineligible individual in subsection 149.1(1) of the Income Tax Act is replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a record expiry has occurred under the Criminal Records Act,

Definition.Text: ineligible individual, at any time, means an individual who has been

Paragraph.Label: (a)
Paragraph.Text: convicted of a relevant criminal offence unless it is a conviction for which

Subparagraph.Label: (ii)
Subparagraph.Text: a record suspension has been ordered under the Criminal Records Act and the record suspension has not been revoked or ceased to have effect,

Subsection.Text: Subsection 149.1(1.01) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.MarginalNote: Deeming rule â Safe Streets and Communities Act
Subsection.Text: In this section, a reference to a record expiry is deemed also to be a reference to a pardon that is granted or issued under the Criminal Records Act.
In this section, a reference to a record suspension is deemed also to be a reference to a pardon that is granted or issued under the Criminal Records Act.

Section.Text: Subsection 10(8) of the DNA Identification Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Despite anything in this section, stored bodily substances of a person in respect of whom a record expiry under the Criminal Records Acthas occurred shall be kept separate and apart from other stored bodily substances, and no such bodily substance shall be used for forensic DNA analysis, nor shall the existence of such a bodily substance be communicated to any person.

Despite anything in this section, stored bodily substances of a person in respect of whom a record suspension, as defined in subsection 2(1) of the Criminal Records Act, is in effect shall be kept separate and apart from other stored bodily substances, and no such bodily substance shall be used for forensic DNA analysis, nor shall the existence of such a bodily substance be communicated to any person.

Section.Text: Paragraph 36(3)(b) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: inadmissibility under subsections (1) and (2) may not be based on a conviction in respect of which a record expiry has occurred under the Criminal Records Act or in respect of which there has been a final determination of an acquittal;

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 43:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of subsection 36(3):

Subsection.Text: The following provisions govern subsections (1) and (2):

Paragraph.Label: (b)
Paragraph.Text: inadmissibility under subsections (1) and (2) may not be based on a conviction in respect of which a record suspension has been ordered and has not been revoked or ceased to have effect under the Criminal Records Act, or in respect of which there has been a final determination of an acquittal;

Section.Text: Paragraph 53(f) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the effect of a record expiry under the Criminal Records Act on the status of permanent residents and foreign nationals and removal orders made against them; and

ExplanatoryNote.Label: Clause 44:
ExplanatoryNote.ExplanatoryText: Existing text of relevant portions of section 53:

Section.Text: The regulations may provide for any matter relating to the application of this Division, and may include provisions respecting

Paragraph.Label: (f)
Paragraph.Text: the effect of a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act on the status of permanent residents and foreign nationals and removal orders made against them; and

Section.Text: Paragraph 82(1)(d) of the Youth Criminal Justice Act is replaced by the following:

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may consider the finding of guilt in considering an application for conditional release or any application under the Criminal Records Act.

Subsection.Text: Subject to section 12 (examination as to previous convictions) of the Canada Evidence Act, if a young person is found guilty of an offence, and a youth justice court directs under paragraph 42(2)(b) that the young person be discharged absolutely, or the youth sentence, or any disposition made under the Young Offenders Act, chapter Y-1 of the Revised Statutes of Canada, 1985, has ceased to have effect, other than an order under section 51 (mandatory prohibition order) of this Act or section 20.1 (mandatory prohibition order) of the Young Offenders Act, the young person is deemed not to have been found guilty or convicted of the offence except that

Paragraph.Label: (d)
Paragraph.Text: the Parole Board of Canada or any provincial parole board may consider the finding of guilt in considering an application for conditional release or for a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act.

Section.Text: Subparagraph 119(1)(n)(iii) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: considering an application for conditional release, or any application under the Criminal Records Act, made by the young person, whether as a young person or an adult,

Subsection.Text: Subject to subsections (4) to (6), from the date that a record is created until the end of the applicable period set out in subsection (2), the following persons, on request, shall be given access to a record kept under section 114, and may be given access to a record kept under sections 115 and 116:

Paragraph.Label: (n)
Paragraph.Text: a member of a department or agency of a government in Canada, or of an organization that is an agent of, or under contract with, the department or agency, who is

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: considering an application for conditional release, or for a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act, made by the young person, whether as a young person or an adult,

Section.Text: Subparagraph 120(4)(c)(iii) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: considering an application for conditional release, or any application under the Criminal Records Act, made by the young person after the young person becomes an adult.

Subsection.Text: If a young person was found guilty of an offence set out in the schedule is, during the period of access to a record under subsection (3), found guilty of an additional offence set out in the schedule, committed when he or she was a young person, access to the record may be given to the following additional persons:

Paragraph.Label: (c)
Paragraph.Text: a member of a department or agency of a government in Canada, or of an organization that is an agent of, or is under contract with, the department or agency, who is

Subparagraph.Label: (iii)
Subparagraph.Text: considering an application for conditional release, or for a record suspension under the Criminal Records Act, made by the young person after the young person becomes an adult.

Section.Text: Subsection 128(5) of the Act is replaced by the following:

Subsection.Text: Despite subsections (1), (2) and (4), an entry that is contained in a system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police to match crime scene information and that relates to an offence committed or alleged to have been committed by a young person shall be dealt with in the same manner as information that relates to an offence committed by an adult for which a record expiry has occurred under the Criminal Records Act.

Despite subsections (1), (2) and (4), an entry that is contained in a system maintained by the Royal Canadian Mounted Police to match crime scene information and that relates to an offence committed or alleged to have been committed by a young person shall be dealt with in the same manner as information that relates to an offence committed by an adult for which a record suspension ordered under the Criminal Records Act is in effect.

Section.Text: The Pardon Services Fees Order is repealed.
